summary: Data professionals make a critical mistake by leading conversations with data and technology rather than understanding business problems first. The business cares about making money, not data itself—data is merely a means to an end. Success requires learning to speak stakeholders' language and genuinely understanding their needs before proposing data solutions.
key_points: Data professionals suffer from the 'streetlight effect'—focusing on data (where they're comfortable) instead of learning stakeholder needs (where the real value is)
The business cares about making money; data only matters when it helps achieve that goal
Technical expertise is necessary but insufficient—communication and problem discovery skills are equally critical
Building feedback loops with stakeholders through regular check-ins and satisfaction metrics creates alignment
The data-business divide won't close until data professionals stop approaching problems from a data-first viewpoint
money_quotes: The business doesn't care about data. The business cares about making money.
When I ask for the time, don't tell me how to make a watch. Just tell me the time.
Data is just a means to an end. As a data professional, the sooner you realize this, the longer and more successful you'll be in this industry.
Stop leading with data in your discussions with the business. Learn and speak the language of your stakeholders.
key_insight: Data professionals must flip their approach: instead of leading with data and technology, start by deeply understanding business problems in stakeholders' own language, then determine if and how data can help.
